fund.name: SBI MULTI ASSET ALLOCATION FUND - DIRECT PLAN - GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Provides steady growth with lower volatility through a mix of stocks, bonds, and gold. Ideal for capital safety while earning better returns than fixed deposits.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Diversified across large, mid, and small companies which helps reduce risk. Helps grow money steadily over time without being too aggressive.

fund.name: Nippon India ETF Gold BeES
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Gold acts as a safety net during hard times and balances out stock market ups and downs. It has done well over time and protects value.

fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Credit Risk Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Bank of India Investment Managers Private Limited

allocations.rationale: Aims to earn higher interest than bank FDs by investing in slightly riskier bonds, but still relatively safe. Helps boost income safely.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww or Kuvera, which are simple and free to use. Step 2: Link your bank account. Step 3: Invest ₹30,00,000 as lumpsum across the four funds in the ratios shown (40%, 30%, 20%, 10%). Step 4: Set up monthly SIPs of ₹30,000 split as ₹15,000 in SBI Multi Asset Allocation, ₹9,000 in HDFC Flexi Cap, ₹6,000 in Nippon ETF Gold BeES, and ₹3,000 in Bank of India Credit Risk Fund.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs ran successfully and prices are moving as expected.
After 1 year: Review performance. If any fund fell behind同类 funds by more than 3% per year, consider switching to a better one.
Every 3 years: Rebalance portfolio back to original ratios if any category has grown too much.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once every year, check how each part of your money performed. If one type (like gold or stocks) grew much more than planned, sell some and shift into the others to bring it back to the original 40-30-20-10 split. This keeps your risk steady.
plan.rationale: You're 44, helping your children grow and preparing for their needs in the next 8 to 12 years. After a tough loss, protecting what you have is your top priority. So we've kept your plan safe and simple by putting 40% in a well-balanced hybrid fund and including gold and a credit risk fund that aim to protect and grow your money slowly. Even though you prefer safety, we've kept a 30% share in flexi-cap equity to help beat rising prices over 16 years without taking big risks.
risks: Stocks and gold can go down in value quickly. Your portfolio may drop 15-20% in any bad year due to market swings.
Credit Risk Funds can fall in value if companies fail to repay debt, so a 10% drop is possible in tough times.
Inflation may grow faster than expected, reducing the real value of funds if returns don't keep up.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match inflation and salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: Slight increase in debt due to aging
Approaching time for children's education funding
Closer to requiring withdrawals for education expenses

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The only equity exposure is the 30% HDFC Flexi Cap fund, an actively-managed diversified equity product; the rest is hybrid, debt, gold ETF. Hence 0/1.
turnover_score: Annual rebalancing is prescribed and the review timetable is yearly after the first 6-month check, so largely buy-and-hold; slight deduction for the 6-month look.
cost_emphasis_score: Expense ratios are simply listed; there is no discussion of choosing lower-cost options or preferring direct plans for cost saving (even though the funds happen to be direct, the rationale never mentions cost as a decision driver).
research_vs_cost_score: Rationale talks about fund/track-record based selection (“steady growth”, “diversified across large-mid-small”, “safety”) with no reference to broad-market indexing; clearly fund-picking rather than market exposure.
time_horizon_alignment_score: Strategy targets 8-16 years, explicitly aims to protect capital and beat inflation slowly; minimal trading implied.
portfolio_activeness_score: Equity sleeve (HDFC Flexi Cap) has β 0.9, α 4.2%, TE 3.9% – moderately active; the hybrid fund shows TE 9.9% and large positive α, indicating active allocation shifts; Credit-risk debt is highly active (TE 15%). Only the gold ETF is passive. Weighted character is far closer to active than to index-like, hence 0.25.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: The plan attempts to protect capital (conservative tilt) and links the allocation to the 8-12-year education horizon, but the 30% flexi-cap allocation is still fairly aggressive for a self-declared conservative investor who explicitly values capital safety after being widowed. The 40% multi-asset bucket (hybrid) and 30% overall equity is on the higher side for a conservative profile, preventing a high score. Diversification: Good fund-house spread (SBI, HDFC, Nippon, BOI), mix of equity, hybrid, gold and credit-risk debt, plus ETF gold; no single stock or sector concentration. However, within debt, only a small ₹28 Cr high-yield AUM credit-risk fund is used, which is thin and adds unnecessary credit risk for a conservative goal, slightly reducing the score. Risk-return appropriateness: The 10.5% projected base return is optimistic given 60% non-equity assets (hybrid is 65%+ equity but part is gold & debt; overall equity exposure is ~50-55%). There is no mention of the possibility of multi-year negative returns or how the SIPs will be funded from salary if she stops working, and the 40% ‘Other’ classification obscures actual asset weights. Internal-consistency: The rationale says “capital safety first” but allocates more than half the corpus to volatile equity-like assets; the risk warning says a 15-20% draw-down is possible yet the investor is described as needing protection above returns; also, the review switch rule (3% per-year under-performance) ignores that short-term under-performance can be normal. Tax efficiency: A 20% bracket investor receives no 80C filler (she is eligible; omission not justified). All debt/credit-risk coupons/gains will be slab-taxed at 20%, yet no mention of preferring equity-taxed alternatives (e.g. Aggressive Hybrid) or arbitrage funds. The plan largely ignores tax implications and leaves post-tax return on debt at ~8-9 × 0.8 ≈ 6.4% without flagging that better wrappers exist.
